*Boswell audit — BannermanLeasing — 2026-05-23 05:10*

## Is This App Working?

The short answer is: it works, but only barely, and only under the right conditions. Think of it like a car that runs fine on a smooth road but will stall the moment anything unusual happens.

The app is called BannermanLeasing, and its main job seems to be generating a year's worth of content (like social media posts or marketing materials) using AI tools like ChatGPT or Claude. It then stores those 365 pieces of content and lets someone manage them.

The problem is that the app was built with a very fragile setup. The process that packages the app for publishing (called a "build pipeline" — think of it like the assembly line that puts the finished product in a box) can silently break without telling anyone. If one file is missing, you get a broken app with no warning message. You'd have no idea something went wrong.

There's also a serious setup problem: the instructions tell you to copy a template file that doesn't actually exist. It's like a recipe that says "refer to the ingredient list on page 12" and page 12 is blank. This means anyone trying to run or maintain this app from scratch hits a wall immediately.

Perhaps most importantly for you personally: the app needs about 10 different passwords and secret codes (called "API keys" and "environment variables") to connect to outside services like the AI providers and the database. None of these are set up or documented in the code. Without them, the app quietly does nothing — no error message, no explanation. It just fails silently. This means if you handed this to someone to maintain, they'd be guessing in the dark.

There is a backup/recovery option (the hosting service keeps old versions of the app automatically), but nobody wrote down how to use it. That's like having a fire extinguisher with no instructions.

## Is It Safe?

This is the most urgent section. **The app has serious security problems that could cost you real money if it were exposed to the internet right now.**

The biggest issue: the app acts as a middleman (a "proxy") between users and paid AI services like OpenAI or Anthropic. Every time someone uses the AI features, it charges against API keys (think of these like a credit card connected to the AI service). The app is supposed to require a password before letting anyone use those features. But the way the password check is written, if the password hasn't been set up yet — which is currently the case, since no passwords are configured — the app skips the check entirely and lets *anyone* use it. Any stranger on the internet could use your AI services and run up charges on your bill.

To make this worse, the app is also configured to accept requests from any website anywhere on the internet (this is called "wildcard CORS" — think of it as leaving every window in a building open). Combined with the missing password, this means the app is essentially an open door to a service you're paying for.

There's also a very powerful database password (called a "service role key" — it bypasses all the normal security rules and gives total access to all your data) being used in a way that could accidentally expose it. If that key leaked, someone would have full control over all the app's stored content.

On top of all this, four known security vulnerabilities (think of these like known lock-picking weaknesses) have been found in the software tools the app was built with. They've been publicly disclosed and have official tracking numbers. None are catastrophic on their own, but they haven't been patched.

**What this means for you:** If this app is currently live and accessible on the internet, there is a real risk that strangers could use it to rack up charges on connected AI accounts. You should find out immediately whether it's publicly accessible, and if so, take it offline or get technical help urgently.

## Is the Code in Good Shape?

No — but this is more about long-term maintenance difficulty than immediate danger. Think of it like a house that's livable but has a lot of DIY work done by someone who knew what they were doing in some rooms and cut corners in others.

The code was clearly written by one person, fairly quickly, without a second person reviewing it (a "code review" — like having someone proofread an important letter). There are only two saves in the entire six-month history (called "commits" — like save points in a video game). That's a sign this was written in one or two big bursts with no ongoing maintenance.

One specific problem worth explaining plainly: there's a critical piece of logic — the function that generates content for the AI — that was copied and pasted word-for-word into two different places in the code. If someone ever needs to fix or update that logic, they have to remember to do it in both places. Miss one, and the app behaves inconsistently in ways that are very hard to diagnose.

The most alarming design choice for you as the owner: the app stores a full year of generated content in something called "sessionStorage" (a temporary memory space in a browser tab). This means if the browser tab is closed, the browser crashes, or someone opens the app in a private/incognito window, all 365 pieces of generated content are permanently deleted with no warning. For a tool whose entire purpose is managing a year-long content calendar, this is like storing your only copy of an important document on a Post-it note.

There are also no automated quality checks — no spell-checker equivalent for code, no automatic tests that verify the app behaves correctly before changes go live. The only check is whether the app can be packaged at all, not whether it actually works.

## What the App Looks and Feels Like

The app appears to be a single-screen dashboard (imagine a sidebar menu on the left and a main content area on the right) that shows a 365-day content calendar. When you open it, it immediately shows all 365 tasks, which is a nice touch — you're never looking at a blank screen.

However, the experience falls apart when things go wrong, which happens a lot. If the AI fails to generate content, if you've hit a usage limit, or if your internet connection blips, the app doesn't show you a friendly error message. Instead, it either shows nothing, shows a blank panel, or crashes silently. There's no "something went wrong, here's what to do" screen.

There's also no setup guide built into the app itself. A new user opening it for the first time with nothing configured would have no idea what to do or why nothing is working.

The app likely doesn't work well on phones or tablets. It uses a layout style (sidebar + content pane) that typically breaks on small screens unless someone specifically designed it for mobile, and there's no evidence that was done here.

## What Should I Do With This App?

- **Find out immediately if the app is live on the internet.** If it is, it may be costing money right now by allowing anyone to use the AI features for free. Ask a technically-minded friend or a freelance developer to check whether the app's web address is publicly accessible. If it is, taking it offline temporarily is the safest move while you figure out next steps.

- **Don't try to run or set up this app yourself without technical help.** The setup process is broken in a way that stops even experienced developers. Handing this to a developer and asking them to spend 5–10 hours cleaning up the security and setup problems would be a reasonable first step before anyone uses it.

- **Locate and secure any accounts connected to this app.** Look for accounts with OpenAI, Anthropic, Google AI, and a service called Supabase. Change the passwords and API keys (the secret codes used to bill for AI usage) on all of them. This limits your exposure regardless of what you decide to do with the app.

- **Decide whether this app has ongoing value before investing in repairs.** The security problems are fixable, but fixing them properly — plus cleaning up the code quality issues — would take a skilled developer roughly two to four weeks of work. If the app was generating real business value (saving hours of content creation work per week), that investment could make sense. If it was an experiment or a side project, it may not be worth the repair cost.

- **If you decide to keep the app, ask any developer you hire to fix the security issues first, before anything else.** Specifically: the open AI proxy, the wildcard CORS setting, and the Supabase master password exposure. Everything else can wait. Those three things together are the equivalent of leaving the front door open on a building with a billing account inside.
